sentences.text: Blancha worked from the underground.
And she was married to a Van Oste, O-S-T-E-N.
He was one of the highest generals in the Belgium army, worked for the underground.
During the daytime, he worked with the Germans.
And at night, he sent messages to England.
He helped over 100 Jews.
My mother knew Blancha.
From where, I don't know.
But when Blancha heard that we are in danger at the Adams' she started looking for a place for us.
We have to remember that the Belgian people weren't ready to help so easily.
I don't know if I mentioned it, but if I didn't, it will be repeated.
There were 60,000 Jews in Antwerp.
And 5,000 remained alive.
And I'm one of the 5,000.
And now I remember that I said that.
There were very few Belgiums who wanted to put their life in danger to help.
Blancha was one of those who did, and Van Oste, too.
Blancha looked for a place for us.
And before she found a place at the Adams', [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] she-- we went to [?
Beam ?] Street.
Beam ?] Street was an empty house.
Downstairs was a prostitute home.
So that mean-- meant that we had to be-- even if the empty house, we had to be very, very quiet.
Because the Germans downstairs shouldn't hear that somebody is at the attic.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We were there for quite a while.
I don't remember how long.
And then we again had to move.
I forgot-- I guess it was because I didn't want to talk about it.
When we were at the Adams', my daddy ran out of money.
And-- but he still had enough to go to one of his friends, non-Jewish friends, at night to exchange diamonds.
He still had some diamonds.
And when he got to his friend, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the friend decided that he wants the diamonds, but he doesn't want to pay for it.
So instead of taking away the diamonds when my daddy slept over there, he gassed my daddy.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The next day--
The next day, a police who worked for the underground came to tell us.
My mother took my daddy's prayer shawl [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and told the police to make sure that he is buried with that, that he's put-- that it's put around him.
We could not go to the funeral.
Because if we would have gone to the funeral, then we would have been killed.
What he told us is that the casket will be in a truck.
And he can make sure that the truck should go through the house where we are hiding so that we can see the casket.
We're all saying we saw the casket.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess I did.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My daddy was buried in the cemetery, in a Christian cemetery.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My mother [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] said after years of liberation that she cannot die if she doesn't rebury my daddy in a Jewish cemetery.
When my son was-- I don't know-- younger-- that must've been six or eight years old, my husband, my son, my mother went to Antwerp.
And we reburied my daddy in a Jewish cemetery.
Now Antwerp doesn't have-- Belgium doesn't have Jewish cemeteries.
It has a no man's land between Holland and Belgium.
And there is one place there that those who were killed during the war in-- are buried on one-- in one grave.
My daddy has, though, a separate grave.
Because we reburied him.
And he has a stone.
And I remember my mother saying, now I can die.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We needed mon-- sorry.
We-- first of all, my, my younger brother, Elie-- my younger brother-- my only brother who was with me in hiding-- he, he really didn't want to live.
He wanted to go outside and be killed.
He used to say, why do I have to live?
I don't want it anymore.
I can't take it-- day and night in a little room, not allowed to even talk loud, and so on.
My mother used to say, Kinder, children, [GERMAN SPEECH].
We must live so we can retell.
The world won't believe it.
But the meantime, when my daddy died, my mother lost hope of living, too.
My mother was a big smoker then.
She smoked cigarettes.
And I remember my mother used to say, I don't want food today.
I want a cigarette.
And I remember rolling cigarettes for my mother, the tobacco and the thing.
How she finally got those things, I don't know.
But she had a cigarette rolling.
When daddy died, she stopped.
She didn't ask for that cigarette.
And we were very much concerned.
Because we knew that if mother doesn't want to smoke, she's very, very sick.
She's not going to live.
And I remember after a week-- or I don't know how many days, because the days went into nights, the nights went into days-- she said, Clara, roll me a cigarette.
And that was one of my beautiful things in hiding.
My mother asked for a cigarette.
And we decided that we're going to go on and try and survive.
And we did go on.
That's when Blancha came.
To.
Beam ?] Street, and in [?
Beam ?] Street to that horrible place.
Prostitute.
And by the way, it was an old house.
But if you were able to-- the windows were black, painted black.
But there was a little thing through the window, you know, where it wasn't all painted black.
And you looked out.
You saw something beautiful.
And I remember myself going there many, many times when I was a kid and hiking there.
It was the [?
Skelder ?], where it used to be the third port in the world.
It's magnificent.
And here, I could live a little bit with memory.
But in the other hand, I lived with saying, why was I able to go out then and not now?
What's the difference from now to then?
And I, of course, had an answer but didn't have an answer.
The answer was, I was born Jewish.
I'm different.
And the, the non-answer was, where is the world?
Why did they let this happen?
Were there any other people in hiding there?
Not there, not there-- but we were in certain places that I will mention where other people were in hiding with us.
Right.
Right.
We were in spring of '43.
Kroll.
Kroll helped us.
And--
Kroll was a very, very fascinating person.
He was a very, very poor person.
And I just saw Kroll again.
He's 92 years old.
He was brought up in one of the most poorest sections in Antwerp.
They have farms there.
But they're little farms, very, very poor.
Kroll used to say he's a commie, he's a communist.
But Kroll was the heart of an angel.
And he, too, helped a lot of Jews.
He never wanted to be-- after the war-- recognized.
He didn't want his name in the Yad Vashem.
But I tried very hard.
He didn't want anything.
And he's still very, very poor.
Last time I saw Kroll in his home, there wasn't a chair that you really could sit down on-- very, very poor.
So I said, to him, Kroll, what do you want me to do for you?
Anything you want, just say it.
And I would have.
I would have sold my shoes even.
So he looked at me.
And he said, you did it.
You're alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, before I left back for America, I left chairs there.
I mean, he couldn't send them to America.
So he had to accept it.
But this was Kroll, a very uneducated person but a good-hearted person and a very poor person.
So Kroll took us, first of all to a-- we had to leave that place.
And he took us to a bar.
And we were there for a few days or a week.
When we [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] left the bar, he took us to a farm, to a very, very-- really, we slept with the cows.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Now, it's easy to say, took us.
We went.
But I want you to know that one night, for example, neighbors told that we are at Kroll's.
And the Gestapo came in from one door.
And my mother threw us out from the window.
So that's how we survived.
If my mother wouldn't have thrown us out from the window, we wouldn't have been-- I wouldn't have been here.

It was very, very difficult for me, and I'm sure for my sister and my brother, my younger brother too, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to be in a farmhouse, to be in a place where the sun shines outside, where there's a little river, where there's fresh air, and where you can't make a move.
You have to lie there with the cows on top.
You have to wait til they bring them in and milk them.
That's how we got a little milk.
And you have to spend there day and night and night, and day.
And you don't know what the end will be.
You start wondering, really, is it worthwhile?
On the other hand, I never wanted to die.
I wanted to live.
I felt I had a lot to give to life.
And my mother used to say to my little brother, who every day was ready to walk out to be killed, watch Clara.
You'll see what she's doing.
Just watch her.
Finally, my mother got something for my brother that was good.
I don't know where she got a chess set.
And she played a lot of chess with him.
And I'll never forget.
I don't know which place in hiding, mother lost her game to my brother.
And I think that she could never forgive herself.
And, and we kept on saying she did it purposely.
And she said, no.
I didn't.
I just lost.
He really knows.
I taught him too good.
And this was one of our little vignettes of hiding.
I'd like to remind the world of the Warsaw ghetto uprising and where I was when this happened in 1943.
I was also in a hiding place at Mrs.
Brussels', a very chic home, three-story home.
Every luxury you want was in that house.
Even now, you would call it a very upper, upper middle class home.
She lived alone there.
This Mrs.
Brussels had-- she wasn't Jewish.
And she had a husband who was Jewish.
He was taken by the Germans at the [? bursa ?], the story I told.
She felt-- she was very much afraid, but she did.
But she couldn't-- she kept saying, my conscious.
I won't be able to die if I don't do it.
She decided to take in a family and hide them.
And she took us in when we had to run from one place to the other.
And she took us in.
And she hid us on the third floor.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My, my sister, who loved to play the piano and hadn't played the piano for quite a while, went downstairs one day and played the piano.
Mrs.
Brussels' neighbors got to know that she's hiding Jews.
And one neighbor went to the Gestapo and told.
They rang the bell.
We had signs that if somebody comes, we should know.
And we knew that the Gestapo was in the house and that my sister is sitting near the piano, playing the music.
They didn't even look at my sister.
They asked Mrs.
Brussels where those Jews are.
Now, Mrs.
Brussels that day had a toothache.
And she had the scarf around her face.
And she looked very sick.
And she said to them, I have no Jews.
I'm not hiding Jews.
And my sister is sitting at the piano.
And they, of course, said, we have to search the house.
They searched the house.
They were at our door on the third floor.
And she said to them, you have been all over already.
What do you think, you wouldn't have found anybody?
And one of the SS men said in German, let's go.
And they went down.
And they left-- another incident that only destiny can explain.
And if you're a very religious person, only God can explain.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
The, the help of getting in was one person only knew from the other.
Because if the Germans would catch you, let's say, and you were hiding Jews, then they didn't-- and, and they would torture you, then you would tell about three, four hiding places.
So it was like a chain of command.
Only one knew about the other.
Now, before we got to Mrs.
Brussels', we were in five, six places only for three, four days.
Because there was a bell someplace that rang and said, they know you're here.
So I, I only remember myself taking my coat, or my jacket, or whatever sweater, and mother saying, kinder, children, we must go.
And we went from one place to the other.
And the underground helped.
Our most important people were Blancha, Kroll.
During the hiding, one day a priest-- he wasn't a priest.
He was a Jew who was dressed as a priest-- came to us and said that they are letting Romanian Jews live in peace in Antwerp and that we should go out of hiding.
When we heard that, we were very excited about it.
And my mother too.
Now, this Jew who was dressed as a priest had contact with priest and nuns.
And mother said, let's try it.
She took a chance.
And they brought us to a house where they called it the nuns priest home.
It was like an empty home near a church.
And we weren't allowed to walk out.
That they told us not to do.
But we can sit at a window and look out.
And that was already a very, very big thing.
Because you see the daylight.
And you're sitting freely at the window.
But one day, my mother realized that her passport had run out.
And she had to go to the [? stuthouse ?] to renew the passport.
Yes-- and-- no.
She was in Brus-- Brussels.
She went to Brussels.
I'm sorry I said.
By train-- she went alone by train.
I'm sorry I said Antwerp.
She went by train to Brussels and she-- to renew the passport.
And she left us messages.
I'll get to one of them.
She left us quite a few messages.
When we figured out that mother should be back, mother wasn't back.
And we started being very much afraid what's happening.
Mother knew that we are worried.
And she had one of the underground people, who I am sure helped her to get to, to Brussels, because how did she get a ticket, or how did she-- she never told us.
I don't know.
And the man came.
And he said, don't worry.
Your mother is all right.
She's going to come back.
Well, when she came back, she told us the horror she had in that office.
The Germans nearly kept her.
They called her all kinds of names, this and that.
And they nearly kept her.
When she got back-- also, one of the nuns and the priest, the Jewish priest, said to her, Rose, you got the passport.
But it's time to hide again.
I think they are taking Romanian Jews again.
And we went into hiding again.
It was after.
No.
No.
It was before.
I'm sorry.
Before.
I'm--
Yes.
It was-- I'm sorry.
Go ahead.
Yes
We were at Mrs.
Brussels'.
And after this incident with the SS man, she felt that we were safe.
They had been here.
And for a while, we were safe.
Now, we did not have radio, or we don't-- forget about everything else-- no papers, no nothing.
When day during the Warsaw-- the, the first up-- the day of the-- of April 14, 1943, the Warsaw-- she came up with a radio.
And she said, I want you to listen to this.
And we listened to the BBC radio-- England, or course.
And it was describing the Warsaw ghetto uprising.
When we listened to this, we felt that tomorrow, the world is going to liberate every Jew.
And my mother kept on saying, this is going to be the Passover of Passovers, because we were liberated.
Moses took us out of Egypt.
And now, the world is going to take us out of Egypt from Hitler.
But it didn't happen.
The Jews fought for a month there.
They fought most-- the strongest army in the world.
They, of course, at the end, lost.
The Warsaw ghetto was burned.
And we listened to all of this.
And then we really lost hope.
Because we said, now after the whole world knows about this and nobody says anything, then there's no hope.
And even my mother that I don't remember too often saying anything that wasn't hope, she said many times during that 30 days, where is America?
And where are the American Jews?
